title: Memory - bits, bytes, and addresses
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: Everything a computer stores is bits (0 or 1). Eight bits make a byte. Memory is one enormous array of bytes, each with a numbered address.
explanation: Under all the languages and pictures, a computer holds only two things: 0 and 1, a bit. Eight of them group into a byte, which can hold a number from 0 to 255 - enough for a letter, part of a colour, a piece of anything. Memory (RAM) is just a vast row of these bytes, each at a numbered address, like houses on an endless street. A 'variable' is really a friendly name for one of those addresses. Everything else - text, images, programs - is a way of arranging bytes.

exercises.question: How many bits are in a byte?
Memory (RAM) is best pictured as...
options: a huge array of bytes, each with an address
hints: Each byte sits at a numbered spot.
A huge array of addressed bytes.
